preFix.title: Fitting kitchen drawer runners
preFix.subtitle: Replace worn drawer runners with modern side-mounted slides
preFix.excerpt: How to replace wooden drawer runners in a kitchen cabinet with modern metal side-mounted slides: measuring, marking the cabinet, screwing the cabinet-side rail, fitting the drawer-side rail, and adjusting for smooth running and level alignment.

content.text: A kitchen drawer that sticks, drops at the front, or requires lifting to close usually has worn wooden
drawer runners

. Replacing them with metal side-mounted ball-bearing slides gives a smooth, self-closing action and a weight capacity that wooden runners cannot match. The job requires only basic measuring and drilling.
When to use it
Drawer drops or sticks when opened.
Drawer must be lifted to close.
Drawer comes out of the cabinet entirely when opened fully.
What you need

A pair of 450 mm (or matching drawer depth) side-mounted ball-bearing drawer slides. Measure the internal depth of the cabinet and the depth of the drawer to confirm the slide length. Most kitchen replacement slides are 450 mm for standard 500 mm-deep cabinets.
Step-by-step
Step 1, remove the old runners.
Remove the drawer. Unscrew or pull off the old wooden runners from both the cabinet sides and the drawer sides.
Step 2, mark the cabinet sides.

Measure the height of the drawer side and mark the centre point on the cabinet wall. Standard placement puts the slide centreline at the midpoint of the drawer height, or slightly below on heavy drawers. Mark a level horizontal line at this height on both cabinet walls, using a combination square or spirit level to ensure the lines are level and equal on both sides.
Step 3, fix the cabinet rails.

Separate the slide into its two parts (cabinet rail and drawer rail). Fix the cabinet rail to the cabinet wall along the marked line, screwing through the slotted holes. Leave the screws slightly loose until the drawer has been tested for alignment, then tighten fully.
Step 4, fit the drawer rails.

Fix the drawer rail to the side of the drawer at the same height from the drawer bottom. Clip the drawer onto the cabinet rails and test: it should slide freely, sit level, and close flush. Adjust via the slotted fixing holes until the drawer face aligns perfectly, then tighten all screws.
Common mistakes
Cabinet rails not level.
A rail fitted slightly out of level makes the drawer tilt as it closes. Check level before tightening and compare the height of both cabinet rails.
Drawer rails fitted at different heights.
Both drawer rails must be at exactly the same height from the drawer bottom to prevent the drawer binding. Measure from the bottom of the drawer on both sides.
